title: Barn Door Cabinet or Pantry
summary: A tall shelved pantry with a sliding barn door on a track — big storage behind a single sliding face, no swing clearance needed.
description:
A sliding barn door is the right answer when you want a lot of storage in a spot where a swinging door would hit something. This is a full-height pantry: a tall 1x12 carcass with ten adjustable-position shelves and a center divider, faced with a sliding tongue-and-groove barn door on a track.

The carcass is straightforward pocket-hole casework, but two things make or break it. First, square: a cabinet this tall amplifies any racking, so check the diagonals before the face frame and back go on, because after that it's locked in whatever shape it's in. Second, the door and its track are a system — the track hardware determines the door's height and how far it hangs, so install the hardware first and measure the actual opening it creates before you build the door to fit. Building the door to the drawing and hoping the hardware agrees is how you end up with a door that drags or leaves a gap.

A nice production tip from the source: paint the face frame and the carcass separately before joining them. Cutting in around an installed face frame is slow and fiddly; two flat pieces spray or roll far faster.

tools.note: Ripping the tongue-and-groove edges off the door's outer boards.

materials.name: Barn door track hardware kit
materials.note: Buy and install FIRST — it sets the door's height and clearance.

cutList.note: Rip the T&G edges off the first and last boards. Build to fit your hardware.
Cut to the door width your hardware dictates.

steps.title: Build the carcass box
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes on the ends of the top and bottom boards and attach to the sides with glue and 1-1/4" pocket screws, hiding the pockets on the outsides. Hold the bottom up 3-1/2" for the toe space. Attach the 1x4 base support under the bottom shelf with pocket holes, then secure with nails and glue.

steps.title: Install shelves and check square
steps.body:
Drill 3/4" pocket holes on each end of every shelf and attach to the sides and center divider with pocket screws and glue. Cut a 12" spacer block to set consistent shelf heights.

Before the face frame and back go on, check the cabinet for square by measuring the diagonals — a tall cabinet locks in whatever shape it has once those are attached.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame separately with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, then attach to the front with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails. It's often easier to paint the face frame and the carcass separately, then join the pre-painted parts.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Mark the shelf locations on the back side edges, apply glue, and set the back panels on. Nail the back to every shelf, side, and divider with 3/4" brad nails — nailing it to the shelves is what keeps a tall cabinet from racking.

steps.title: Install hardware, then build the door to fit
steps.body:
Install the barn door track hardware first and measure how big the door actually needs to be — the hardware and how it mounts determine the door's height and clearance.

Build the door from 1x6 tongue-and-groove boards, ripping the T&G edges off the first and last boards on the table saw, with 1x8 across the top and bottom. Hang it, adjust the rollers so it glides without dragging, and finish. Add the 1x6 header and 1x2 crown above the opening.

images.alt: A tall pantry cabinet with a sliding tongue-and-groove barn door on a track
